metadata.raw_response_text:
The pool opened Memorial Day weekend, though the water stayed bone-cold until July. Every morning at eleven, Mrs. Gable would heave open the heavy metal gate of the William J. Henderson Municipal Pool, and the line would shuffle forward—kids clutching dollar bills damp from their pockets, teenagers flicking cigarette butts into the hydrangeas, mothers carrying infants in car seats balanced on one hip. The admission was two dollars for residents, four for everyone else, and nobody checked IDs too closely. This was how Middletown worked: you were who you said you were, until you weren’t.

The pool sat at the bottom of a natural bowl in Lincoln Park, surrounded by oak trees that dropped helicopter seeds into the water by August. It was kidney-shaped, a 1960s leftover, painted a fading robin’s-egg blue that had chalked to gray in the shallow end. The concrete deck was spiderwebbed with cracks where dandelions pushed through. When I was ten, I believed the pool was enormous; at sixteen, working the snack bar, I realized you could cross it in twenty strokes. But size wasn’t the point. The point was that it was the only place in town where the temperature dropped ten degrees, and the only public space where you could lie down without someone asking you to move along.

What the pool revealed about Middletown wasn’t immediately obvious. From the highway, we looked like any other post-industrial Ohio town—brick bungalows, a shuttered JCPenney, a mural of a cardinal painted on the water tower. But standing on the lifeguard stand, you could see the topography of our divisions mapped out in beach towels.

The deep end belonged to the Black kids from the east side, who arrived in coordinated packs and claimed the diving board with the territorial efficiency of a military operation. They brought Bluetooth speakers that played Detroit hip-hop, and they stayed until closing, their fingertips pruning to white. The shallow end was territory for the white mothers from the north side, who set up nylon cabanas and applied sunscreen with the solemnity of religious ritual. Their children wore floaties until age eight and ate organic cheese sticks from insulated bags.

Between them, in the transitional four-foot section, floated everyone else: the Mexican families who walked over from the rental duplexes on Maple, the elderly white men who did water aerobics in cargo shorts, the teenage girls from the trailer park who shared one tube of lip gloss and took turns floating on their backs, staring at the sky.

The pool was supposed to be the great equalizer. Entry was cheap; modesty was impossible. We all wore the same drugstore sunscreen, smelled the same chlorine in our hair. But equality required maintenance, and Middletown had stopped maintaining much of anything. The filtration system groaned. The bathroom stalls lacked doors. By mid-July, the water developed a metallic shimmer that the town manager called “algae bloom” but looked to me like rust.

One afternoon in August, the pump broke. The water went stagnant overnight, turning the color of weak tea. The town council debated closing the pool for the season—three weeks early—to save the repair costs. What happened next revealed the true architecture of the place.

Mrs. Gable, who had worked the gate for thirty years and knew every child’s name and parentage, organized a meeting. Not at the town hall, but at the pool itself, at dusk, when the heat broke. Two hundred people showed up. The white mothers from the north side brought petitions. The Black teenagers from the east side brought their older brothers who worked at the auto plant. The Mexican families brought tamales wrapped in foil, enough to feed everyone.

They spoke in shifts. A man who worked maintenance at the hospital explained centrifugal pumps. A twelve-year-old girl read an essay she’d written for school about learning to swim. An elderly Black woman recalled swimming in this same pool in 1964, the year they finally removed the “Whites Only” sign, and how the water had felt exactly the same—cool, shocking, merciful.

The pool stayed open. The town found the money. A mechanic from the high school fixed the pump for free, using parts scavenged from the vocational ed department.

I think about that meeting whenever I drive past Lincoln Park now. The pool is still there, still fading, still cracking. Middletown itself continues its slow economic contraction—more storefronts empty, more pills circulating, more young people leaving. But the pool remains a kind of truth-telling device. It shows who we are when we are nearly naked, when we are bored, when we are hot and irritable and forced to share space. It reveals that we are not as divided as our neighborhoods suggest, nor as equal as our myths insist. We are simply people who need cool water in August, who will stand together in the chemical smell of chlorine to demand that the gates stay open, that the water keeps running, that there remains one place where the only thing required of you is the ability to float.
